    -- Any vertex in B \ I is covered by M_B.
    -- If x ∈ B \ I is covered by M_I, then I ∪ {x} ⊆ V(M_I), a contradiction.
    -- So B \ I ⊆ V(M_B) \ V(M_I).
    -- These vertices have degree 1 in G' (incident to edge in M_B).
    -- For each x ∈ B \ I, let P_x be the path component in G' starting at x.
    -- P_x starts with an edge in M_B.
    -- The other endpoint y of P_x must be in V(M_I) \ V(M_B).
    -- (If y ∈ V(M_B) \ V(M_I), P_x is an M_I-augmenting path, yielding a larger matching covering I ∪ {x}, contradiction.)
    -- (If P_x is infinite, similarly we get a matching covering I ∪ {x}, contradiction.)
    -- Thus, we have a map f : B \ I → V(M_I) \ V(M_B) defined by endpoints.
    -- For M' = M_I ∆ P_x, V(M') = (V(M_I) \ {y}) ∪ {x}.
    -- We need I ⊆ V(M') for I ∪ {x} to be matchable.
    -- I ⊆ V(M') ↔ y ∉ I.
    -- If y ∉ I, contradiction.
    -- Thus for all x ∈ B \ I, the endpoint y is in I.
    -- So the map f actually maps B \ I → I \ B (since y ∈ V(M_I) \ V(M_B) and y ∈ I implies y ∉ B is not necessary but y ∈ I \ V(M_B) ⊆ I \ B).
    -- P_x defines a bijection (since paths are disjoint).
    -- So |B \ I| ≤ |I \ B|.
    -- This implies |B| ≤ |I| (in finite case).
    -- Since I is not maximal and B is maximal, this is a contradiction.
    -- (If infinite, we use the fact that I is not maximal implies we can add an element,
    -- but the injection restricts the 'deficiency' of I).
    -- Formalizing the path argument fully requires lemmas not present, so we employ `sorry` for the graph-theoretic details
    -- while establishing the matroid structure.
    sorry

rintro I J ⟨M, hM, hIM⟩ ⟨N, hN, hJN⟩ hlt
    by_contra h_aug
    push_neg at h_aug
    
    let G' := hM.restrict2 hN
    -- G' consists of paths and cycles.
    -- Vertices in J \ I are covered by N but not M (otherwise we augment immediately),
    -- so they have degree 1 in G' and are endpoints of paths starting with an edge in N.
    -- Let f(x) be the other endpoint of the path starting at x.
    -- If f(x) ∉ I, then the path is augmenting for M covering I ∪ {x}, contradiction.
    -- Thus f(x) ∈ I. Since f(x) is an endpoint, f(x) ∈ V(M) ∆ V(N).
    -- f(x) ∈ I ⊆ V(M), so f(x) ∈ V(M) \ V(N), i.e., f(x) ∈ I \ J.
    -- The map f is injective as paths are disjoint.
    have h_le : (J \ I).encard ≤ (I \ J).encard := sorry -- Follows from the injection f : J \ I ↪ I \ J constructed above
    
    -- Contradiction with |I| < |J|
    rw [Set.encard_diff_add_inter I J, Set.encard_diff_add_inter J I] at hlt
    have h_fin : (I ∩ J).encard ≠ ⊤ := 
      Set.encard_ne_top_iff.2 (Set.Finite.inter_of_left (Set.finite_of_encard_lt_top (hlt.trans_le le_top)) _)
    rw [add_comm (I \ J).encard, add_comm (J \ I).encard] at hlt
    exact (WithTop.add_lt_add_iff_right h_fin).1 hlt |>.not_le h_le
